verses.text: The sons of Judah; Pharez, Hezron, and Carmi, and Hur, and Shobal. And Reaiah the son of Shobal begat Jahath; and Jahath begat Ahumai, and Lahad. These are the families of the Zorathites. And these were of the father of Etam; Jezreel, and Ishma, and Idbash: and the name of their sister was Hazelelponi: And Penuel the father of Gedor, and Ezer the father of Hushah. These are the sons of Hur, the firstborn of Ephratah, the father of Bethlehem. And Ashur the father of Tekoa had two wives, Helah and Naarah. And Naarah bare him Ahuzam, and Hepher, and Temeni, and Haahashtari. These were the sons of Naarah. And the sons of Helah were, Zereth, and Jezoar, and Ethnan. And Coz begat Anub, and Zobebah, and the families of Aharhel the son of Harum. Guidance: After tracing the royal line all the way to the post-exilic generations in chapter 3, the Chronicler now circles back to fill in the remaining families and clans of Judah — the wider tribe beyond the kingly line. These are the lesser-known households: the Zorathites, the families around Etam and Bethlehem and Tekoa, the sons of Hur 'the father of Bethlehem.' The chapter is showing us the Chronicler's concern for the whole tribe of Judah, not only its famous kings. After lifting our eyes to David and Solomon and Zerubbabel, he stoops again to record the obscure clans, the ordinary families who made up the body of the tribe. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD's people are not only their great names; the covenant community is composed of countless ordinary households, each one known and numbered by God. The chapter is teaching the returned exiles that every Judahite family — even those who could claim no king or prophet in their line — had a place and a name in the record of the people of God. The mention again of Bethlehem and Tekoa (the home town of the prophet Amos, and of the wise woman who pleaded with David) roots these clans in the familiar towns of Judah. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD values the whole, not only the prominent; in His book the names of the lowly clans of Judah stand recorded as carefully as the names of kings, for He is the God of all His people, great and small.

And Jabez was more honourable than his brethren: and his mother called his name Jabez, saying, Because I bare him with sorrow. And Jabez called on the God of Israel, saying, Oh that thou wouldest bless me indeed, and enlarge my coast, and that thine hand might be with me, and that thou wouldest keep me from evil, that it may not grieve me! And God granted him that which he requested. Guidance: Suddenly the dry genealogy opens into a jewel of a story — two verses of pure gospel light. Jabez bore a name that meant sorrow ('Because I bare him with sorrow'), a name that spoke of pain and difficult beginnings; yet he became 'more honourable than his brethren.' And the secret of his honour was prayer: 'Jabez called on the God of Israel.' His prayer is a model of believing, dependent petition — that God would bless him indeed, enlarge his border, keep His hand upon him, and keep him from evil so that it might not grieve him. And the verse ends with the sweetest words: 'And God granted him that which he requested.' The chapter is showing us, set like a gem amid the lists of names, that the LORD hears and answers the prayer of faith. A man marked from birth by sorrow rose above his circumstances not by his own strength but by calling on the God of Israel. The chapter is teaching us that no one is bound by a sorrowful beginning or a hard name; the believer who lays hold of God in prayer can rise to honour and blessing. The chapter is teaching us how to pray — humbly ('Oh that thou wouldest'), believingly (he expected God to act), and for the things that matter: God's blessing, God's enlarging of our usefulness, God's hand upon us, and God's keeping us from the evil that would grieve us. Jabez did not ask merely to be kept from pain, but from sin ('keep me from evil') — the truest enlargement is to be kept holy under the hand of God. The chapter is teaching the post-exilic community, and us, that the God who recorded countless names also stoops to hear one man's cry; He is not too great to be moved by the prayer of the lowly. The God who granted Jabez his request is the same God who invites us still: 'Call upon me in the day of trouble: I will deliver thee' (Ps 50:15). Among all the names that merely pass by, Jabez is remembered forever because he prayed — and was heard.

And Chelub the brother of Shuah begat Mehir, which was the father of Eshton. And Eshton begat Bethrapha, and Paseah, and Tehinnah the father of Irnahash. These are the men of Rechah. And the sons of Kenaz; Othniel, and Seraiah: and the sons of Othniel; Hathath. And Meonothai begat Ophrah: and Seraiah begat Joab, the father of the valley of Charashim; for they were craftsmen. And the sons of Caleb the son of Jephunneh; Iru, Elah, and Naam: and the sons of Elah, even Kenaz. And the sons of Jehaleleel; Ziph, and Ziphah, Tiria, and Asareel. And the sons of Ezra were, Jether, and Mered, and Epher, and Jalon: and she bare Miriam, and Shammai, and Ishbah the father of Eshtemoa. And his wife Jehudijah bare Jered the father of Gedor, and Heber the father of Socho, and Jekuthiel the father of Zanoah. And these are the sons of Bithiah the daughter of Pharaoh, which Mered took. And the sons of his wife Hodiah the sister of Naham, the father of Keilah the Garmite, and Eshtemoa the Maachathite. And the sons of Shimon were, Amnon, and Rinnah, Benhanan, and Tilon. And the sons of Ishi were, Zoheth, and Benzoheth. The sons of Shelah the son of Judah were, Er the father of Lecah, and Laadah the father of Mareshah, and the families of the house of them that wrought fine linen, of the house of Ashbea, And Jokim, and the men of Chozeba, and Joash, and Saraph, who had the dominion in Moab, and Jashubilehem. And these are ancient things. These were the potters, and those that dwelt among plants and hedges: there they dwelt with the king for his work. Guidance: The genealogy resumes its catalogue of Judahite clans, and among the names two things stand out. First, the note that Othniel descended from Kenaz — Othniel was the first of the judges, the kinsman of Caleb who delivered Israel (Judg 3:9-11); and 'Caleb the son of Jephunneh,' the faithful spy who wholly followed the LORD, appears here too. Second, a striking detail: 'the sons of Bithiah the daughter of Pharaoh, which Mered took' — an Egyptian princess married into Judah, another foreigner grafted into the covenant people. And the passage repeatedly honours the working families: the craftsmen of the valley of Charashim ('for they were craftsmen'), those who wrought fine linen, and 'the potters, and those that dwelt among plants and hedges: there they dwelt with the king for his work.' The chapter is showing us that the tribe of Judah was a fabric of many kinds of people — deliverers and judges, foreign converts honored in the record, and humble tradesmen who served the king with their hands. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD records not only warriors and rulers but the linen-weavers, the potters, the gardeners — the working people whose labor served the kingdom. Their craft was their calling, and the Chronicler dignifies it: 'there they dwelt with the king for his work.' The chapter is teaching us that all honest labor done in the LORD's service is honorable and remembered by God; the potter at his wheel and the gardener among the hedges have their names in the sacred record alongside the judges and the kings. And the recurring inclusion of outsiders — an Egyptian princess woven into Judah — teaches again that the covenant people were enriched by those who came in from the nations. The phrase 'these are ancient things' invites the reader to treasure these old records as a heritage; the LORD's dealings with the lowliest families of Judah, in their towns and trades, are worth remembering.

The sons of Simeon were, Nemuel, and Jamin, Jarib, Zerah, and Shaul: Shallum his son, Mibsam his son, Mishma his son. And the sons of Mishma; Hamuel his son, Zacchur his son, Shimei his son. And Shimei had sixteen sons and six daughters; but his brethren had not many children, neither did all their family multiply, like to the children of Judah. And they dwelt at Beersheba, and Moladah, and Hazarshual, And at Bilhah, and at Ezem, and at Tolad, And at Bethuel, and at Hormah, and at Ziklag, And at Bethmarcaboth, and Hazarsusim, and at Bethbirei, and at Shaaraim. These were their cities unto the reign of David. And their villages were, Etam, and Ain, Rimmon, and Tochen, and Ashan, five cities: And all their villages that were round about the same cities, unto Baal. These were their habitations, and their genealogy. Guidance: The Chronicler turns from Judah to Simeon, the tribe whose territory lay within Judah's southern borders. A telling note is sounded: Simeon's families 'had not many children, neither did all their family multiply, like to the children of Judah.' Simeon, whose ancestor had been rebuked by Jacob for violence and scattered in Israel (Gen 49:5-7), dwindled while Judah flourished — and indeed Simeon was eventually absorbed into Judah and disappears as a distinct tribe. The chapter is showing us the contrast between the tribe that increased and the tribe that diminished; the LORD's blessing of fruitfulness rested differently on different families. The chapter is teaching us that there is a sovereign and sometimes sobering difference in the fortunes of God's people: Judah, the tribe of the scepter, multiplied and endured; Simeon faded. The old word over Simeon — that he would be scattered in Israel — quietly worked itself out in history, even as the old word over Judah — that the scepter would not depart — was being fulfilled. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD's purposes, declared long before, come to pass in the slow unfolding of generations; the patriarch Jacob's blessings and rebukes were prophecies that history confirmed. Yet Simeon is not forgotten: the Chronicler carefully records the tribe's towns and villages, its place within the land. The chapter is teaching us that even the diminishing tribe, the family that did not multiply, has its honored place in the record of God's people — the LORD does not despise the small or the fading, but numbers them still among His own.
